one_liner: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with 5 days of bloody diarrhea, followed by decreased urine output and pallor today.

stage2.hpi_extension: Five days ago he began having watery diarrhea that progressed to grossly bloody stools over the following days along with crampy abdominal pain. The diarrhea has now mostly stopped, but over the past 24 hours he has become pale, lethargic, and has produced very little urine. His mother notes puffiness around his eyes. He attended a family barbecue about a week before symptoms began, where he ate a hamburger that may have been undercooked. Two other children at the gathering also had diarrhea. He has had no true neurologic symptoms and no fever in the past 48 hours.
stage2.pmh: Born at term, previously healthy, vaccinations up to date
stage2.medications: No antibiotics (mother specifically did not give any). No NSAIDs.
stage2.family_history: No bleeding disorders, no renal disease, no hemolytic anemia
stage2.social_history: Lives at home with parents and 2 siblings, attends preschool, no recent travel
stage2.physical_exam: General: tired, pale 4-year-old boy. HEENT: pale conjunctivae, periorbital edema. Cardiovascular: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs: clear. Abdomen: soft, mildly diffusely tender, no rebound, bowel sounds present, no hepatosplenomegaly. Skin: scattered petechiae on lower legs, no purpura, no rash. Extremities: mild bilateral ankle edema. Neuro: sleepy but arousable, no focal deficits, no seizures, non-toxic.

lab_results.result: Hgb 7.2 g/dL (low), platelets 42,000/uL (low), WBC 14.5 (mildly elevated with left shift). Reticulocyte count elevated. Smear: numerous schistocytes (fragmented RBCs and helmet cells), polychromasia, thrombocytopenia. No blasts.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia plus thrombocytopenia is the hallmark of a thrombotic microangiopathy. In a child following bloody diarrhea, HUS is the leading diagnosis.

lab_results.result: Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (markedly elevated for a 4-year-old), BUN 58 mg/dL, K+ 5.8 mmol/L (high), bicarbonate low. LDH markedly elevated, indirect bilirubin elevated, haptoglobin undetectable. PT, PTT, and fibrinogen NORMAL (ruling out DIC). Urinalysis: proteinuria and hematuria with red cell casts.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Acute kidney injury plus laboratory evidence of intravascular hemolysis (elevated LDH, elevated indirect bilirubin, undetectable haptoglobin) completes the HUS triad. Normal coagulation parameters distinguish HUS from DIC, and the renal predominance and age distinguish it from TTP.

lab_results.result: Stool culture: positive for Shiga-toxin-producing Escherichia coli O157:H7. Shiga toxin assay on stool: positive. Stool ova and parasites negative. C. difficile negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Confirms typical (diarrhea-associated, D+) HUS caused by Shiga-toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7, classically acquired from undercooked ground beef. Shiga toxin binds to Gb3 receptors on glomerular endothelium, damaging the microvasculature and initiating the thrombotic microangiopathy.

stage5.final_clue: ADAMTS13 activity returns normal, distinguishing HUS from TTP. He is managed supportively with IV fluids, electrolyte correction, and one session of hemodialysis for oliguria and hyperkalemia. Antibiotics are deliberately AVOIDED. His renal function and platelet count recover over the following 2 weeks.

debrief.classic_presentation: Typical (diarrhea-associated, D+) HUS classically occurs in young children several days after an episode of bloody diarrhea, often traced to undercooked ground beef, unpasteurized milk or juice, or contaminated produce. It presents with the triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes, elevated LDH, undetectable haptoglobin), thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury (rising creatinine, oliguria, hematuria, proteinuria). Unlike TTP, neurologic symptoms are typically minor or absent. Atypical (non-diarrheal, D-) HUS is caused by dysregulation of the alternative complement pathway and is treated with eculizumab.
debrief.pathophysiology: Shiga toxin produced by E. coli O157:H7 (the classic cause of typical HUS) -- or by Shigella dysenteriae -- is absorbed from the inflamed gut and binds to globotriaosylceramide (Gb3) receptors on glomerular endothelial cells. The toxin inhibits protein synthesis and triggers endothelial injury, exposing the subendothelial matrix, activating platelets, and leading to microthrombus formation in the glomerular and other microvasculature. Red cells are fragmented (schistocytes) as they are sheared through these thrombi. ADAMTS13 is NORMAL in HUS -- the distinguishing laboratory finding from TTP.
debrief.treatment_overview: Management is largely SUPPORTIVE: IV fluids and electrolyte management, blood transfusions as needed, and dialysis for severe renal failure. CRITICALLY, antibiotics and antimotility agents should be AVOIDED in children with suspected Shiga-toxin-producing E. coli because they may increase toxin release and INCREASE the risk of HUS. Plasma exchange is generally not indicated in typical (diarrhea-associated) HUS, although it is the treatment for TTP. Atypical HUS (complement dysregulation) is treated with eculizumab, a monoclonal antibody against C5.
high_yield_pearls: HUS triad = microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + acute kidney injury. Classic history: young child with bloody diarrhea after eating undercooked ground beef.
Cause: Shiga-toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 (most common) or Shigella dysenteriae. Toxin binds Gb3 on glomerular endothelium.
HUS vs TTP: HUS has more prominent renal injury and follows bloody diarrhea in children; TTP has more prominent neurologic symptoms in adults. ADAMTS13 is NORMAL in HUS, LOW in TTP.
Avoid antibiotics in suspected STEC infection -- antibiotics can increase toxin release and worsen HUS risk. Antimotility agents also contraindicated.
Atypical HUS (complement dysregulation, not preceded by diarrhea) is treated with eculizumab (anti-C5 monoclonal antibody).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) -- Hematology and Oncology chapter, 'Platelet disorders / Thrombotic microangiopathies -- Hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS)' section.
